key_insight: Residual stress in a thin film can be predicted by treating relaxation as a dislocation pile-up at the film-substrate interface, yielding a stress gradient that depends strongly on geometry and the initial stress profile.
engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanics-based way to estimate residual stress redistribution in constrained thin films, which is important for reliability, interface integrity, and process design.
method: Model plastic strain through dislocation density and derive a fundamental stress-density relation.
Apply force balance to obtain a singular integro-differential equation for arbitrary non-uniform residual stress.
Solve the equation numerically with a collocation method for constant, linear, parabolic, and exponential initial stresses.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical solutions and compares multiple initial stress profiles, showing that residual stress relaxation and dislocation populations vary with thickness-to-width ratio and load shape.
watch_item: The model assumes an idealized impenetrable interface and requires both positive and negative Burgers vectors in equilibrium, so practical implementation may be sensitive to real interface conditions and material complexity.
paper_title: Residual stress gradient in a thin film within the dislocation pile-up theory
